Post / Idea: I spent months building an AI-powered calorie tracking app. Today I'm sharing the journey publicly. Every win, every failure, every $0 revenue update. Day 1. #buildinpublic
I tried MyFitnessPal, Lose It, Cronometer, and FatSecret. None of them could understand 'one plate chicken machboos with raita.' So I built my own. Meet TrackKcal.
The tech stack behind TrackKcal: React Native + Expo, Supabase, GPT-4o-mini for food analysis, TypeScript everything. Total monthly cost to run: ~$12. #buildinpublic #indiehacker
Hot take: calorie tracking apps shouldn't need you to scroll through 47 search results to log 'eggs on toast.' You should just type it and be done. That's what TrackKcal does.
Day 5: Revenue: $0. Users: 0. Lines of code: 24,000+. Sleep last night: 4 hours. Would I do it again? Absolutely. #buildinpublic
What's the most annoying thing about calorie tracking? A) Too many taps to log food B) Can't find local foods C) Inaccurate portions D) Forgetting to log

Here's something wild — the UAE fitness app market is worth $90.8M and growing at 14.1% per year. Yet there isn't a single nutrition app built specifically for Middle Eastern food. That's the gap I'm filling. #madeindubai

One feature I'm proud of: voice logging. You literally say 'I had two eggs, toast with butter, and a cup of coffee' and the AI breaks down every calorie and macro. No scrolling. No searching. Just talk.

Controversial opinion: MyFitnessPal has 200 million users and still thinks 'biryani' is one food. There are 15+ types of biryani. Hyderabadi biryani has different calories than Lucknowi. This is the problem I'm solving.
Actual conversation with my AI calorie tracker: Me: 'half a plate of lamb ouzi with yogurt on the side' AI: 680 cal, 42g protein, 38g fat, 45g carbs. Try doing that with a barcode scanner.

Today I built local food caching — when you log a meal, TrackKcal remembers it. So next time you type 'my usual breakfast,' it loads in 1.2 seconds instead of calling the AI. Small wins. #buildinpublic

Most calorie tracking apps are built on the USDA food database — a dataset of American foods. If you type 'machboos' into MyFitnessPal, you'll get wildly inaccurate results (if any). This is the core problem TrackKcal solves. We use AI to understand food descriptions in context — cuisine, portion sizes, cooking methods. It's a fundamentally different approach.

I chose to build TrackKcal with React Native + Expo instead of going native. Here's why: one codebase, 90% code sharing between iOS and Android, faster iteration. For a solo founder, this was the right call. The 10% of platform-specific quirks I deal with is worth the 50% time saving.

The UAE health and wellness market is booming. But here's what I've noticed: most apps in this space are imports — built in Silicon Valley, adapted for the region as an afterthought. There's an opportunity to build products that are local-first, not local-adapted.

Founder lesson: I spent a week perfecting a feature nobody asked for. Then I showed a 30-second voice logging demo on TikTok and got more engagement than anything else. Build what excites people, not what excites your inner engineer.
